utterance: Oh yeah, actually! So last week was kind of hectic—I had this project deadline at work that just ate up like Tuesday and Wednesday, so I completely abandoned my usual routine of prepping on Sunday. Normally I've got it all mapped out, you know, grocery list ready to go, but that week I just... didn't. So I ended up doing this weird thing where I was basically stopping at the store after work like three times, which is so inefficient, I know. But it actually forced me to be more flexible with what we ate—like I'd see what looked good that day and just grab stuff for dinner that night. Some of it turned out better than expected, honestly. My partner was like "finally, we're not having the same rotisserie chicken situation," but also it was stressful because I felt kind of scattered the whole time. I guess it made me realize that my normal system works pretty well for me when life's not crazy, but when things get chaotic I just kind of... wing it. Which isn't ideal but it happens, you know?
label: yes

utterance: Yeah, so it was definitely more than just the shopping, you know? Like, it wasn't even about going to the store—I could've done that. It was more that the whole system just fell apart, right? Without that Sunday prep time blocked out, I didn't have my meals planned, so I was constantly thinking about what to eat throughout the week. Then you're stressed at work, you're hungry, and suddenly you're making bad choices or spending way more money grabbing stuff last minute. It was like this cascade effect where missing one thing meant everything else got messier. The stress wasn't really the shopping trip itself—it was knowing I'd lost control of the whole week.
label: no

utterance: Yeah, I mean... like, it just takes the stress out of it, you know? And I can actually buy the right stuff at the grocery store instead of just grabbing random things and half of it going bad. Plus my partner and I can actually coordinate – like, if he knows we're doing tacos on Wednesday, he can let me know if he wants to pick up something extra or whatever. It just... makes everything smoother, I guess.

The thing is, I spend like an hour every week just *planning* what I'm going to buy in the first place. I'll stand in my kitchen with my phone, scrolling through recipes, checking what's on sale, trying to figure out what actually goes together so I'm not wasting money on ingredients that'll just sit there. That's where the real frustration is — it's not about the price when I'm at the store, it's that I've already spent so much time before I even get there trying to make it all work. I keep coming back to this — if I could just... I don't know, have a system that actually helped me plan properly, I wouldn't be second-guessing myself in the aisle in the first place.

utterance: *leans forward slightly* Yeah, well, it saves time, obviously. You're not wandering around like an idiot looking at every product. But honestly, that's not even the biggest part of it for me. *gestures more animatedly* The real thing — and I think people miss this — is that my kid has severe food allergies. Like, life-threatening severe. So when I go to the store without a plan, that's when mistakes happen. That's when I'm stressed, I'm rushed, I'm not reading every single ingredient label carefully enough, and one wrong item could send us to the hospital. *voice becomes more intense* So yeah, having a system means I know exactly what I'm buying before I walk in. I've already checked everything at home, cross-referenced against his allergen list, verified it hasn't changed formulations. The store is just... execution. Pick it up, pay, leave. No room for error that way. That's why the system matters so much. It's not really about convenience or whatever — it's about keeping my kid safe. Everything else is secondary to that.
label: yes

utterance: *shifts slightly in seat* Yeah, I mean... I guess it depends on the product. If it's something I like, I'm annoyed. If it's not, whatever. What I keep coming back to is the waste — like, all the meal planning apps and services I've tried, they just... they don't account for what's actually in my pantry. I end up throwing out half the ingredients because the plans assume I'm starting from scratch every week. That's the real frustration for me. *leans forward slightly* So when a formula changes, sure, that's annoying in the moment. But what really matters is whether a meal planning system actually reduces waste or just creates more of it. That's what I'm thinking about constantly — I've probably thrown away hundreds of dollars worth of food because the plans don't work with how I actually shop and what I actually have available.

utterance: *shifts forward slightly* Look, I mean... it's just wasteful, right? You buy food, it goes bad, money down the drain. I'm spending like twenty, thirty minutes just standing in front of my fridge going "what am I making?" And then I end up ordering takeout anyway because I'm too tired to think about it.
